quoted_tweet.text:
A Page from History-
History of human thought, philosophy and science, Beginning from the early Greek era:
(An excerpt from my book “The Journey of Light and the Universe, in the Quest of the Reality of Nature”, Chapter 1, pp. 17-19.)

“The Scholastic Era§ with the rise of Greek scholarship around the fifth century BC saw great philosophers and scholars who constituted the first formal accounts that exist of science. 
To begin with, one of the seven Sages of Greece, the great (Phoenician emigrant) philosopher Thales of Miletus (c. 623-545BC), the founder of the Milesian School of thought (and also known as the Father of Geometry), and his illustrious disciple (possibly a prophet) Xenophanes of Colophon (circa 570-478BC), laid down the foundations of logic and philosophy. Their efforts were followed by the Parmenides of Elea (c. 400-500BC), the founder of the School of Elea, and his colleagues, the eminent scholars of the Elean school (the “Eleatics” as they were called), Melissus of Samos (c. 400-500BC) and Zeno of Elea (c. 495-430BC), among others.

All these, collectively, formed the body of early scholastic philosophers hailing from the “Magna Graecia” (or the “Greater Graecian” region), before the advent of the School of Athens#. 

Parmenides and the Thracian philosopher Democritus of Thrace (460-370BC) are perhaps the most prominent pre-Socratic scholars in the history of Greek civilization. Whereas Democritus is known as the Father of Atomic Theory (and possibly of Modern Science as well, because of his pioneering ideas in natural philosophy, mathematics, and geometry), Parmenides is known as the Father of Ontology or Metaphysics. Since it is not possible to reach an in-depth understanding of both of these, these two illustrious names are undoubtedly the two most prominent early beacon houses in the human journey of light. 

The torch of knowledge and truth lit and carried by these two great men was finally taken forward by generations of other Greek philosophers, mathematicians and geometrists, mainly Pythagoras of Samos, Socrates, Plato, and Archimedes, and illuminated the whole world and many ages that mankind saw. Aristotle and physics were merely two of the many fruits of their labor. 

Great kings and emperors, most notably Cyrus the Great (c. 600-529BC), founder of the magnificent Persian Achaemenid empire and well-respected and known all over the world especially by his Persian name, “Kouras Kabeer”, and as “the benevolent and just king” in the words of the great Greek scholar, philosopher and historian Xenophon (c.430-354BC), and then, Alexander the Great, The King of Macedonia (356-323BC), the student of Aristotle and the scholarly and wise emperor who at a young age succeeded Achaemenid empire in ruling over majority of the world, took part in and patronized scholastic endeavors, including establishment of libraries. Beginning, soon, with the great Royal Library of Ashurbanipal, established during the period 668-630BC by the great Assyrian emperor Ashurbanipal (c. 7th century BC) in Nineveh (current Iraq) and the great Library of Alexandria founded by the Roman King Ptolemy I, the former general of Alexander and later the Macedonian ruler of Egypt, in the year 295BC, prominent libraries with collections of several 100,000s of scrolls and cuneiform tablets came into existence.

Democritus suggested the idea of an atom, and soon the three generations of the Greek Scholastic era, commencing with philosopher Socrates of Athens (470-399 BC), followed by his illustrious student Plato of Athens (428-348BC), and Plato’s pupil (and the teacher of Alexander of Macedonia) Aristotle of Stagira (384-322 BC), ended up laying down the foundations of the ancient field of Natural Philosophy, the early form of Physics and physical sciences. It would be unfair not to acknowledge the groundbreaking and meritorious contributions of two great Greek scholars and polymaths Pythagoras of Samos (570-495 BC) and Euclid of Alexandria (~300 BC) to the sciences of mathematics, geometry and trigonometry, which finally culminated in forming the foundations of modern science as we behold today. 

The second century BC (circa 185-128 BC) Bythinia-born and Rhodes-based mathematician and astronomer Hipparchus (or Hipparchos), who spent his whole life in a career of astronomy, is credited 
with the charting of the world’s first complete star map, and thus the first documented proper scientific 
____
# The School of Elea and the works by Parmenides, are mainly known from Plato’s book “Parmenides” and by the Greek historian and biographer of the Scholastic era Diogenes Laërtius (c 200-300AD) [1]. The record also appears in the works by Plato (380-367BC) among others.
____
§ The prominent recorded periods of science can roughly be divided into the Greco-Roman, Arab-Persian, European Renaissance, and Modern Science eras. The Greek scholastic era, especially the schools of Elea and Samoa that preceded the School of Athens, are the most eminent parts of the Greco-Roman era, spanning many centuries [1, 2].
astronomer and probably the true father of scientific astronomy. However, Aristotle shall always be remembered as the first physicist and natural philosopher, who carried out a systematic study of nature, not just physics but biology as well, identified and defined the fundamental elements and devised fundamental physical laws, as well as authored the first book of physics, “Physics”, written in 350 BC. In terms of the first significant contributions to the understanding of light and the field of optics, the fifth-century (~490-430 BC) Sicily-born Greek philosopher Empedocles of Acragas (a city at the coast of the island of Sicily under Greek dominion during antiquities) presented the first theory of light rays, as part of his “Cosmogenic Theory of Four Elements”. He enunciated that the composition of everything was made possible with four basic elements in the universe, which were earth, fire, air, and water, which also collectively made the eye, and this instrument facilitated the interaction of light rays between a source (such as the sun) and objects. He was also the first person to suggest that light had a finite speed and was not an instantaneous magical effect. On the other hand, the well-known Roman philosopher, poet, and one of the flag-bearers of the movement, Lucretius (94-55 BC), though generally not known for these contributions, made remarkable observations on light and colors, in addition to making very deep allusions to the reality. It can be said that before anyone else, he was the first person who delved deeply into the origins of the universe and its form and into the nature of reality. 

The preliminary work by Empedocles was perfected and extended by the Greek pioneering philosopher, mathematician, and father of Euclidean geometry (as well as the founder of geometrical optics) Euclid#, as contained in his detailed treatises on optics and relevant instruments such as mirrors named “Optika” and “Catoptrics”. However, his Magnus opus remains his groundbreaking book on geometry named “The Elements”. Euclid, by virtue of his geometric genius, discussed at length the geometrical properties of light rays and how to calculate angles and distances. It is unfortunate that the origins and period of such a great figure in the history of mankind remain obscure, however, it is known that he was of Greek origin and born around the first quarter of the 4th century BC who spent most of his scientific career in Alexandria soon after the city was found by Alexander at the banks of Nile. He carries the distinguished honor to be remembered in early texts as the “The Author of Elements”. Euclid breathed his last in Alexandria sometime in the first quarter of the 3rd century BC during the reigns of the King Ptolemy I.  After a period of nearly two centuries, a true heir to Euclid was found nowhere else but in Alexandria itself under the Roman empire, in the form of Ptolemy, the great pioneering astronomer, geographer, astrologer, polymath, and musician, whose extensive work in the fields of astronomy, geography and optics left lasting impressions on generations to come in then Roman, Byzantine and Islamic civilizations— serving as one of the major torch-bearers of the light.

The illuminated name of Ptolemy (Ptolemais, or its Latinized form Claudius Ptolemaeus), needs no introduction. The giant star of optics in the medieval era was born around the year 100 AD in the Greek city of Ptolemais Hermiou and spent most of his life in Alexandria, the then center of knowledge, till his death in 168 AD. His treatise on light and optics, called “Optics”, extended the work on geometrical optics by Euclid, with detailed discussions on light rays, media, and the effects of refraction and reflection, becoming the most significant amount of ancient work on optics. His scientific work in the form of Optics and his Magnus opus known as “Almagest” (an Arabic name, owing to its first translation in Arabic) commanded the fields of optics throughout the medieval era. The world-renowned work on optics, the “Book of Optics” by the eleventh-century Arab polymath Alhazen was influenced in part by Ptolemy’s books. Collectively, the three books “Optika”, “Optics” and “The Book of Optics” constitute a treasure trove of light in ancient and medieval times, although unfortunately most of it has been lost.

However, for nearly a millennium no one could make the correct ideas come forth regarding the natural 
____
# Euclid was a contemporary of another great Alexandria-based Greek mathematician and engineer, the Hero (or Heron) of Alexandria— known for many innovations, in particular for his eponymous Heron Formula, for formulating the Principle of Shortest Path for Light, and in designing a steam engine (aeolipile).”

Com todo respeito ao nosso ídolo...

Mas dá pra fazer aquele bingo das entrevistas também com Grafite.

Em todas ele fala a mesma coisa:
Meião, Cesta básica e café da manhã.

quoted_tweet.text:
Ídolo do Santa Cruz, Grafite participou do podcast "Basticast" e comentou sobre algumas situações que viveu no clube antes da aposentadoria. O ex-centroavante comentou sobre a realidade financeira da população que vive no entorno do Arruda, estádio da equipe. 

A condição humilde de cada um, e também a que o Santa vivia, fazia com os torcedores não pedissem camisas, mas sim meiões, pois sabiam que "faria falta", de acordo com o ex-jogador. 

🎥: Reprodução/Basticast
